title: 1  Introduction
text:
This is a book created from markdown and executable code.
See Knuth (1984) for additional discussion of literate programming.

1 + 1

[1] 2

title: 3  Quiz
text:
Match each description with the corresponding R function featured in this chapter.



Returns a quantile of a normally distributed random variable:  — dbinom() pbinom() pnorm() qnorm() prop.table() 


Calculates the total probability from zero to  — dbinom() pbinom() pnorm() qnorm() prop.table() 


Empirical rule validation uses:  — dbinom() pbinom() pnorm() qnorm() prop.table() 


Joint probability tables are produced using:  — dbinom() pbinom() pnorm() qnorm() prop.table() 






Airlines often overbook to hedge against no-shows. For example, if 140 tickets are sold for a 134-seat flight where passengers have an independent 9% chance of not showing up, what is the probability that the airline gets fewer than 6 no-shows?


 0.7%  1.1%  1.6%  2.7%  97%  99%





Suppose that events A and B each have nonzero probability. Determine whether each statement is TRUE or FALSE.



 — TRUE FALSE  When A and B are independent, it follows that P(A | not B) = P(not B).


 — TRUE FALSE  The addition rule simplifies to P(A and B) = P(A) + P(B) when A and B are mutually exclusive.


 — TRUE FALSE  The joint probability P(A and B) is equal to zero as long as A and B are independent.


 — TRUE FALSE  If A and B are complementary, then P(A) = 1 − P(B).






As a major winter storm looms on the radar, a rookie meteorologist notes that there is exactly a 13% chance of snow. If they want the probability it will NOT snow, what rule must they apply?


 addition  delulu  multiplication  negation  rizz  three-month





In a controlled cognitive experiment, participants are asked to press a button as soon as a visual stimulus appears on a screen. Each participant completes many trials. Because reaction time is influenced by many small factors—attention, neural processing speed, fatigue, and momentary distractions—the recorded reaction times vary from trial to trial. Researchers find that reaction times (in milliseconds) for a typical adult under these conditions are approximately normal, with a mean of 250ms and standard deviation of 40ms.



There is roughly a 19% chance that an adult has a reaction time longer than 215ms.


The IQR of reaction times is close to 54ms.


Given that an adult’s reaction time is shorter than 250ms, the probability it is longer than 210ms is approximately 84%



 1 only  2 only  3 only  1 and 2 only  1 and 3 only  2 and 3 only  All  None





Among the great selection of amusements and rides in a typical fair is a quick game of rolling a biased die. The distribution of the die’s faces is tabulated below.


You have to shell out $3 to play the game. The mechanics are simple:



If you land on a face lower than 3, your payout is $12.


If you land on a face higher than 3, you pay the game operator $4.


Otherwise, your payout is $28.



 On average, playing this game many times leads to a small positive gain of about $1 per play.  Since face 5 is the most likely outcome and has a positive payoff, the game must be profitable overall.  The game is fair because the gains and losses are balanced across the different outcomes.  Because more than half of the outcomes lead to a loss, the game must have a negative expected value.  The expected value can be estimated by looking mainly at the most likely outcome (face 5), since it contributes most to the average.  The average result over many plays will be close to zero, so the small gains and losses mostly cancel out.





At the peak of operational pressure, the City of Austin Animal Shelter undertook a comprehensive review of its historical intake and outcome records.


For each animal, two binary variables were recorded: stray and adopted.


To explore the relationship, the analyst computed:

xtabs(~ stray + adopted, data = shelter) %>%
  prop.table(margin = 2) %>%
  round(2)


Which of the following can be correctly inferred from the table?


 1 only  2 only  3 only  1 and 2 only  1 and 3 only  2 and 3 only  All  None

title: Foundations of Data Science
text:
Welcome!!
Dear reader,
This online textbook serves as your first engagement with the concepts and applications of data science. It also introduces you to R and RStudio, which will be the primary computing tool to assist you in making data-driven inferences in practice.
There is little-to-no expectation of you to be excellent navigators of one webpage and one desktop application. So it is highly recommended that as you immerse through this textbook, you read it while copying and executing the code blocks presented in each chapter. Making that a habit for whichever course this reference gets used boosts the chances of the pretension of learning to escape your soul. If you find yourself less inclined to follow that recommendation, then you can just probably go back home and take over your father’s car dealership, or help your mother with grocery shopping.
At any moment you struggle with the coding aspect, reach out to a colleague or to your professor or TA. The sooner you address the hurdle, the better. Your basic recipe for success includes allocating enough time for this and not devote way too much to whatever foolish activities your chapter mates are engaging in.
Take it easy! 😁
\(\small\textit{Dr. H}\) 🐈

title: 3  Probability
text:
3.1 Events, Rules, and Sources of Probability
A probability quantifies how likely an event will happen. The probability of some event is a number between 0 and 1, inclusive. i.e.
\[0\leq P(\textrm{event})\leq 1\]
In this chapter, you will get acquainted with the basics of probability with emphasis on learning how to estimate them using data.
There are three fundamental ways of describing probability in terms of events: simple, joint, and conditional.

title: 3  Probability
text:
3.1.1 Careful Counting
A simple probability is the chance that one event happens. The most common example is the probabilities we can compute from flipping a coin. Understanding the concept of sample space is crucial here, as it determines all possible outcomes of an experiment. In a coin flip, the sample space is {H, T}. Assuming it is a fair coin, then the probability of landing tails is 0.5 because there is one outcome of interest out of two possible outcomes.
This is an application of the counting principle. Assuming all outcomes are equally likely to happen, then
\[\textrm{probability of an event happening}=\dfrac{\#\;\textrm{of outcomes of interest}}{\textrm{total }\#\;\textrm{of outcomes}}.\]
How about a slightly more interesting (and not as boring as coin flipping or dice tossing) scenario? What are the beverage and food preferences of a customer dining at a café?


3.1.2 Estimating Probabilities



3.1.3 Conditional Probability


3.1.4 Negation Rule


3.1.5 Addition Rule


3.1.6 Mutually Exclusive Events


3.1.7 Multiplication Rule


3.1.8 Independent Events

title: 1  RStudio and Data
text:
Hello posit
As planned, we will use R to carry out all of our calculations. Visit posit to download R and RStudio. When you scroll through that link, you should see those two steps beneath.

It is important that R must be installed first, because RStudio will not work without it. RStudio is what we will be opening and using all the time. It is an IDE (integrated development environment), with organized panes where you can write and execute code as well as navigate files and output. Running code in RStudio initiates R to do its main job, i.e. to perform all of the computational work.


Launch RStudio
After successfully setting up R and RStudio, go to your Windows search bar or Mac’s spotlight, type in RStudio then click it. That should launch RStudio and you will see the default IDE with three panes.



Navigating RStudio
That view would suffice if we are only using it as a glorified calculator. As with every topic we would need to do computations, it is ideal that we have a pane where we can create and edit code – we do that by hitting control+shift+N on your keyboard and it will open an untitled script in a new pane called Source.

RStudio has four primary panes:

The Source pane, located top left, allows us to view and edit code-related files (e.g. .R, .rmd, .qmd, .txt).
The Console pane, located bottom left, executes code and displays numerical results.
The Environment pane, located top right, lists currently saved objects (e.g. stored values, imported datasets).
The Output pane, located bottom right, is mainly utilized for managing files, looking at graphs, and browsing documentation of functions and packages through its Help tab.

That is the default pane layout; if along the way, you feel that the Console and Environment panes should switch places or you want the user interface to have a different font, different background color, and all the other aesthetics, the Global Options under Tools can help you make those modifications. The RStudio IDE User Guide has a thorough documentation of such, but for the purposes of keeping it simple, we will stick with the default configuration, except for the tab width (I prefer that it is a width value of 1 – this can be changed through Tools –> Global Options –> Code).


Basic Computations
Ideally, we draft code in the source pane inside an R script (i.e. the Untitled file) and we click Run (ctrl+enter) to execute it. The result will appear in the console. While the console can also do this, it often leads to problematic situations as soon as we write multiple lines of code.

It is good practice that coding is all done in a script, rather than the directly in the console. Describing the contents or practically leaving comments in a lengthy code is also an advantage of using a script versus the console. Comments in the script can be made by putting a pound sign (#) before the line – it does not matter how many, as long as there is at least one, it will turn that line into a comment. This improves overall coding documentation as we learn more concepts that require certain packages and functions.

A common data structure that can be created in R is a vector – a one dimensional collection of values stored in a single object. The concatenate function, c(), creates vectors and doing so requires that the elements inside it are separated by commas. In the example below, they were stored in the environment as an object named scores. Now every time we type scores or call out scores in a function, it will use that particular vector we saved. Of the many built in R functions, mean() is one of them, which in this case was used to obtain the average of their scores.

For demonstration purposes (and as noted earlier), codes can be typed in and executed in the console after hitting enter, but what happened in the scenario below?

Yep, you guessed it right! R is case-sensitive, if we wish to call out scores we must type it as how it is spelled. Object names SHOULD always start with a letter and MUST NEVER have a space. Special characters we can use are underscore (_) and period (.).





Packages
The limitations of R’s default installation are the primary reason packages exist. R packages are collections of functions, datasets, and documentation that extend R’s capabilities by providing additional tools and functionality. Packages are installed from repositories such as CRAN using the install.packages() function or through RStudio’s interface via Tools → Install Packages. Once installed, packages are stored in a library (a directory containing one or more packages) and can be loaded into an R session using the library() function.
For guidance, we will demonstrate the uses of a package by following a series of steps.

Step 1: New Script
On RStudio, create a new script (CONTROL+SHIFT+N).


Step 2: Install and Load a Package
I am a strong advocate of the capabilities of the tidyverse package. The syntax involved in writing code blocks in this package appears more intuitive than that of the default.
On your new script, type this down
install.packages("tidyverse")
and press the Run button (CONTROL+ENTER). After that, type this in the next line
library(tidyverse)
and run it as well. Some of you might see a bunch of messages, usually stating some conflict and such, just go ahead and ignore that. For some, you might just be lucky and not see any of those messages and see that the console is ready again to execute new code.


Step 3: Compare base R and the tidyverse
Let’s do a mini analysis!
At PuthPaws Support Operations, the customer experience team records the handling time (in minutes) for a sample of individual customer support tickets received over a short monitoring period.
The values below represent the time required to resolve seven randomly selected tickets (copy the vector below on your script then run it, so it saves in your environment):
handling = c(12, 15, 9, 20, 18, 14, 16)
Each value corresponds to a single customer support interaction, measured from ticket creation to resolution.

What is the typical handling time?
This pertains to the typical value of a dataset. One way to quantify the typical value is to calculate the mean (or average). In the demonstration earlier, we have already shown that R has a bulit-in function mean that calculates the average for us.
We can solve for the typical handling time by putting the vector handling inside the mean function and running the code:
mean(handling)
And we should get this result in the console
[1] 14.85714
That is pretty straightforward but may get clunky as we stack multiple operators and functions.
Another method is by using the pipe operator (%>%), which becomes available whenever we load the tidyverse package. It allows us to chain multiple lines of code.
As an analog to the computation above, this is how it is done with the pipe operator:
handling %>% 
 mean()
And it should get you the same result. Why do that? Well, it is more intuitive. We can choose to make the output tidier by rounding. If we use the first convention, wrap the round function around the mean function like this:
round(mean(handling), 1)
The round function there takes in two inputs – the number we are rounding and the number of decimal places we are rounding to (in this case, 1). Imagine how this might become more difficult to understand when we wish to use more functions… but with the pipe operator, all we have to do is pipe the next function to the previous (in a logical way) to get to our desired result:
handling %>%
 mean() %>%
 round(1)
Running that yields
[1] 14.9
While the pipe operator has three symbols, there is no need to worry about pressing way too many keys to encode it because there is a shortcut: CONTROL+SHIFT+M – the keystrokes you will remember and appreciate for the entirety of this textbook.



Step 4: Saving a Script
Save your script by going to File then clicking Save (it is a .R file), and save it in wherever folder you know you can easily find on your messy laptop.

title: 4  Quiz
text:
Match each description with the corresponding R function featured in this chapter.



Returns a quantile of a normally distributed random variable:  — dbinom() pbinom() pnorm() qnorm() prop.table() 


Calculates the total probability from zero to  — dbinom() pbinom() pnorm() qnorm() prop.table() 


Empirical rule validation uses:  — dbinom() pbinom() pnorm() qnorm() prop.table() 


Joint probability tables are produced using:  — dbinom() pbinom() pnorm() qnorm() prop.table() 






Airlines often overbook to hedge against no-shows. For example, if 140 tickets are sold for a 134-seat flight where passengers have an independent 9% chance of not showing up, what is the probability that the airline gets fewer than 6 no-shows?


 0.7%  1.1%  1.6%  2.7%  97%  99%





Suppose that events A and B each have nonzero probability. Determine whether each statement is TRUE or FALSE.



 — TRUE FALSE  When A and B are independent, it follows that P(A | not B) = P(not B).


 — TRUE FALSE  The addition rule simplifies to P(A and B) = P(A) + P(B) when A and B are mutually exclusive.


 — TRUE FALSE  The joint probability P(A and B) is equal to zero as long as A and B are independent.


 — TRUE FALSE  If A and B are complementary, then P(A) = 1 − P(B).






As a major winter storm looms on the radar, a rookie meteorologist notes that there is exactly a 13% chance of snow. If they want the probability it will NOT snow, what rule must they apply?


 addition  delulu  multiplication  negation  rizz  three-month





In a controlled cognitive experiment, participants are asked to press a button as soon as a visual stimulus appears on a screen. Each participant completes many trials. Because reaction time is influenced by many small factors—attention, neural processing speed, fatigue, and momentary distractions—the recorded reaction times vary from trial to trial. Researchers find that reaction times (in milliseconds) for a typical adult under these conditions are approximately normal, with a mean of 250ms and standard deviation of 40ms.



There is roughly a 19% chance that an adult has a reaction time longer than 215ms.


The IQR of reaction times is close to 54ms.


Given that an adult’s reaction time is shorter than 250ms, the probability it is longer than 210ms is approximately 84%



 1 only  2 only  3 only  1 and 2 only  1 and 3 only  2 and 3 only  All  None





Among the great selection of amusements and rides in a typical fair is a quick game of rolling a biased die. The distribution of the die’s faces is tabulated below.


You have to shell out $3 to play the game. The mechanics are simple:



If you land on a face lower than 3, your payout is $12.


If you land on a face higher than 3, you pay the game operator $4.


Otherwise, your payout is $28.



 On average, playing this game many times leads to a small positive gain of about $1 per play.  Since face 5 is the most likely outcome and has a positive payoff, the game must be profitable overall.  The game is fair because the gains and losses are balanced across the different outcomes.  Because more than half of the outcomes lead to a loss, the game must have a negative expected value.  The expected value can be estimated by looking mainly at the most likely outcome (face 5), since it contributes most to the average.  The average result over many plays will be close to zero, so the small gains and losses mostly cancel out.





At the peak of operational pressure, the City of Austin Animal Shelter undertook a comprehensive review of its historical intake and outcome records.


For each animal, two binary variables were recorded: stray and adopted.


To explore the relationship, the analyst computed:

xtabs(~ stray + adopted, data = shelter) %>%
  prop.table(margin = 2) %>%
  round(2)


Which of the following can be correctly inferred from the table?


 1 only  2 only  3 only  1 and 2 only  1 and 3 only  2 and 3 only  All  None





A regional food supply company operates three independent sugar processing plants. Due to natural variability in raw material quality and short-term operational fluctuations, the daily output of each plant is modeled as uniformly distributed between 2 and 7 tons.


For planning and cost-control purposes, a plant is classified as operationally compliant on a given day if it produces more than 4 tons of sugar, which is the minimum threshold required to meet distribution commitments without incurring additional logistics costs.


On a randomly selected day, what is the probability that exactly two of the three plants are operationally compliant?


 0.2880  0.3750  0.4320  0.2160  0.6480  0.5184





A large logistics company operates an automated parcel distribution center. One of its critical systems is a high-speed conveyor network whose energy consumption must be carefully managed to avoid overload during peak sorting periods.


An analytics team fits a simple linear regression model using 60 operational snapshots, relating conveyor load intensity (measured in thousands of parcels per hour) to energy consumption (in kilowatts). Instead of reporting a single fitted equation, the team uses a bootstrap procedure with 10,000 resamples to quantify parameter stability and uncertainty.


The resulting bootstrap-based regression summary is reported as follows:

Bootstrap Regression Summary (B = 10,000)

term            estimate   2.5%    97.5%
------------------------------------------------
(Intercept)      42.6      39.8     45.3
load              3.1       2.7      3.5
Residual SD       6.8       6.1      7.6
R-squared         0.74      0.69     0.79


Three operating configurations are then evaluated:



System A: load intensity = 4.0


System B: load intensity = 7.5


System C: load intensity = 11.0



For each statement below, select TRUE or FALSE.



The bootstrap results suggest that energy consumption increases with load intensity, and System C has the highest expected energy consumption among the three systems.  — TRUE FALSE 


The confidence interval for the slope indicates that load intensity is not a statistically meaningful predictor of energy consumption.  — TRUE FALSE 


The relatively high R-squared value indicates that the model explains a substantial proportion of variability in energy consumption, though non-trivial unexplained variation remains.  — TRUE FALSE 


Because the intercept confidence interval is wider than the slope confidence interval, the intercept is more uncertain than the effect of load intensity.  — TRUE FALSE 






Which of the following visualizations requires a sequential explanatory variable (i.e., a variable with a meaningful order such as time or progression)?


 faceted scatterplots  faceted line graphs  filled bar plot  histogram  boxplot  density histogram





A municipal water authority is conducting a detailed audit of residential water usage across a small community in order to better understand consumption patterns and identify potential inefficiencies in infrastructure allocation.


During the preliminary analysis, the data team notes that most households exhibit moderate daily water usage, while a small number of properties—specifically abandoned or unoccupied residences with disconnected service lines—report extremely low recorded consumption values, effectively approaching zero.


To summarize the distribution, the analyst reports the following descriptive statistics:



Mean daily household water consumption: 75 liters


Median daily household water consumption: 82 liters



Based on these summary measures alone, and without assuming any specific parametric form for the underlying distribution, which conclusion is most defensible?


 At least half of all households in the community must have a daily water consumption exceeding 82 liters, with the remaining households necessarily falling at or below this threshold. 
 The distribution is likely characterized by a greater concentration of households below 75 liters than above 75 liters, implying a majority of low-consumption households. 
 The observed relationship between the mean and median is more consistent with a distribution exhibiting left skewness than right skewness, suggesting the presence of a small number of unusually low values pulling the mean downward. 
 The distribution is more plausibly right-skewed than left-skewed, indicating that a minority of households with exceptionally high consumption are likely inflating the mean. 
 At least one household must have a recorded consumption value exactly equal to 82 liters per day, since the median corresponds to an observed data point in the dataset. 
 The interquartile range of the distribution must exceed 7 liters per day, given the observed separation between the mean and median. 





A national public infrastructure agency is conducting a comprehensive review of commuter behavior and service performance across a newly modernized rail network. The dataset contains multiple layers of information collected over one operational year, including passenger counts, delays, station-level usage patterns, and relationships between environmental and operational variables.


A team of analysts is tasked with producing a set of exploratory visualizations for a technical report. Each visualization must be chosen carefully to match the statistical structure of the question being asked, not merely the appearance of the data.


For each analytical objective below, select the most appropriate visualization type.



The operations unit wants to understand how total passenger volume at a major central station evolves over time across the entire year. The emphasis is on identifying gradual trends, seasonal cycles, and persistent upward or downward movements in demand, with the requirement that continuity between consecutive time points must be visually preserved.  — bar plot boxplot histogram line graph scatter plot faceted plot 


A performance audit team wishes to examine the distribution of train delay durations (in minutes) across all trips in the network. Their focus is not on time ordering or grouping, but rather on the shape of the distribution, including skewness, concentration, and the presence of extreme delays.  — bar plot boxplot histogram line graph scatter plot faceted plot 


The regional planning division aims to compare passenger load variability across different station types (e.g., interchange hubs, suburban stops, and terminal stations). The objective is to compare distributions of a continuous variable across multiple categorical groups, emphasizing medians, spread, and potential outliers rather than individual observations.  — bar plot boxplot histogram line graph scatter plot faceted plot 


A systems analyst investigates whether train delays are associated with passenger volume, suspecting that this relationship may differ depending on whether the station is located in an urban core, suburban zone, or rural area. The goal is to visualize a bivariate relationship while preserving interpretability across groups by separating the display into comparable panels.  — bar plot boxplot histogram line graph scatter plot faceted plot 






A national transportation analytics division is preparing an internal technical report on passenger behavior and system performance across a newly expanded rail network. The dataset includes hourly passenger counts, delay durations, station classifications, and relationships between operational load and service efficiency.


Analysts are instructed to choose visualization types that match the statistical objective of each task, with emphasis on whether the goal is to examine distributions, compare groups, or explore relationships between variables.


Complete each statement by selecting the most appropriate visualization type.



To understand how passenger volume evolves smoothly over time at a central hub station, capturing seasonal cycles and long-term trends in demand, the analysts decide to use a  — bar plot boxplot histogram line graph scatter plot faceted histogram faceted scatterplot filled bar plot  to preserve continuity across ordered time points.


To examine the distribution of train delay durations across all trips in the network, with particular attention to skewness, concentration, and extreme delays, without conditioning on any grouping structure, the analysts choose a  — bar plot boxplot histogram line graph scatter plot faceted histogram faceted scatterplot filled bar plot  to visualize the full marginal distribution of a continuous variable.


To compare passenger load variability across different station types (such as interchange hubs, suburban stations, and terminal stations), while emphasizing medians, spread, and outliers rather than individual observations, the analysts select a  — bar plot boxplot histogram line graph scatter plot faceted histogram faceted scatterplot filled bar plot  to summarize and compare distributions across categorical groups.


To investigate whether the relationship between passenger volume and train delay differs across urban, suburban, and rural stations, the analysts construct a  — bar plot boxplot histogram line graph scatter plot faceted histogram faceted scatterplot filled bar plot  to preserve the bivariate structure while allowing comparisons across station categories.






A national railway authority operates two independent monitoring systems that track structural vibration along railway bridges in different regions of the country. Because each region has different engineering characteristics and environmental conditions, each system is evaluated relative to its own historical pattern of readings.


In Region A, vibration readings typically have a mean level of 40 with a standard deviation of 8, while in Region B they typically have a mean level of 100 with a standard deviation of 20.


During a routine inspection, the systems report unusually high readings of 56 in Region A and 132 in Region B.


An engineer is asked to determine which region is currently experiencing a more unusually high vibration level relative to its own historical pattern.


Which conclusion is most justified?


 Region B is more unusual because its reading is larger in absolute terms.  Region A is more unusual because its reading is farther above its mean in raw units.  Both regions are equally unusual because both readings are equally above their means.  Region B is less unusual because larger variability makes deviations less meaningful.  The two regions cannot be compared because they use different measurement systems.  Region A is more unusual because its deviation from the mean is larger relative to its standard deviation.





A national courier company is evaluating four sorting facilities based on daily processing efficiency scores recorded over a fixed operational period. Each facility has the same number of observed days.


The operations team summarizes the data using standard descriptive statistics.


R Output:

Facility                     n    Min   Q1   Median   Q3   Max   Mean   SD
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
Northgate Sorting Hub        20   62    70     75     80    95    76     6
Eastline Distribution Center 20   68    72     74     76    78    74     2
Southport Logistics Yard     20   55    65     78     82    98    75     12
Westbridge Fulfillment Unit  20   70    73     74     75    77    74     1.5



Southport Logistics Yard has the highest variability when measured using:  — SD IQR Both SD and IQR Neither SD nor IQR 


Eastline Distribution Center is best described as:  — High and unstable Moderate and variable Low and stable Outlier driven 


Northgate Sorting Hub has approximately how many observations between Q1 and Q3:  — 5 8 10 15 


Southport Logistics Yard has approximately how many observations above Q3:  — 2 3 5 10 


Which facility is most influenced by extreme values when using SD:  — Northgate Sorting Hub Eastline Distribution Center Southport Logistics Yard Westbridge Fulfillment Unit 


Which facility shows the most consistent performance across both SD and IQR:  — Northgate Sorting Hub Eastline Distribution Center Southport Logistics Yard Westbridge Fulfillment Unit

Time left: 10:00

title: 2  Describing Data
text:
2.2 Fundamental Plots

Distribution of Numerical Variables
You’ve likely seen class test scores summarized with a single number like the mean or median, or glanced at a table of grades and noticed that some values cluster near the top while others are scattered lower down. In many everyday situations—exam results, survey summaries, even sports statistics—this single-number summary is often treated as if it captures the entire story of the data. But it quietly leaves out something essential. Two classes, for example, can share the same average score and still look completely different in reality: one may have most students tightly grouped around the center, while another may contain a mix of very high and very low scores that balance out to the same mean. To understand these differences, we need to move beyond isolated summaries and look at the full structure of the data. This is where the idea of a distribution becomes central. The distribution of a numerical variable describes how all values are arranged across their range—where they tend to cluster, how widely they spread, and whether certain regions contain unusually high or low concentrations of values. Rather than reducing data to a single representative number, it allows us to see the data as an organized whole, where meaning comes from patterns of variation rather than individual observations.
To make these patterns visible, we turn first to graphical representations that reveal the distribution directly rather than describing it indirectly. Among the most fundamental of these are histograms and boxplots. A histogram provides a detailed view of how numerical values are distributed across their range, showing where observations concentrate and where they are sparse. A boxplot, in contrast, offers a more compressed summary of the same distribution, highlighting the median, the spread of the middle half of the data, and the presence of unusually high or low values. Although these two displays differ in how much detail they preserve, they serve the same essential purpose: to translate a list of numbers into a visible structure that can be interpreted at a glance. Together, they form the first step in moving from raw numerical data to meaningful understanding of its distributional shape.
Once the distribution of a numerical variable has been made visible through graphical tools, the next step is to describe its key features in a more compact and formal way. While histograms and boxplots allow us to see shape and structure directly, it is often useful to summarize these patterns using a small set of numerical measures that capture the essence of what we observe. This is where measures of center and spread become important. The mean and median provide two different ways of describing the typical value of a distribution: the mean reflects a balance point influenced by all observations, while the median identifies the central position of the data in a way that is less affected by extreme values. Alongside these measures of center, we also need ways to describe variability. The standard deviation captures the overall degree to which values deviate from the mean, while the interquartile range focuses specifically on the spread of the middle portion of the distribution. Together, these summaries do not replace the visual representation of a distribution; rather, they condense it, allowing us to compare and communicate its most important features more efficiently while still remaining grounded in the structure revealed by the plots.

Z-scores
While graphical displays and numerical summaries allow us to understand the overall shape, center, and spread of a distribution, they do not always answer a more focused question: where does a particular value stand relative to the rest of the data? A score of 80, for example, is not inherently high or low unless it is interpreted in the context of its distribution. In some datasets it may be exceptional, while in others it may be quite ordinary. To make such comparisons meaningful, we need a way to express values on a common scale that accounts for both the center of the distribution and its variability. This leads to the idea of standardization. A z-score measures how far a value lies from the mean of its distribution in units of standard deviation, allowing different observations—across different datasets or even different contexts—to be compared on a consistent scale. In this way, z-scores shift our focus from raw values to relative position within a distribution, completing the progression from describing data, to summarizing it, to interpreting individual observations within its structure.



Comparing Group Summaries
While numerical summaries allow us to condense and compare distributions of quantitative data, not all data behaves in the same way. Many important variables are categorical in nature, describing group membership rather than numerical magnitude—such as departments, regions, or types of outcomes. In these cases, the focus shifts from describing how values are spread along a number line to understanding how observations are allocated across distinct groups. This is where bar plots become essential. A bar plot translates categorical data into a visual form by displaying the frequency or proportion of observations within each category, making comparisons across groups immediately visible. Rather than summarizing shape or spread in a continuous sense, it emphasizes differences in magnitude between discrete categories. In this way, bar plots extend the same underlying idea of distribution to a different kind of data, allowing us to see how observations are distributed across groups rather than along a numerical scale.


Relationship Between Numerical Variables
As we move beyond the description of a single variable or the comparison of separate groups, the focus of data analysis shifts toward understanding how variables relate to one another. In many real-world contexts, data is not observed in isolation; instead, each observation contains multiple measurements that may be connected in meaningful ways. To explore these connections, we use graphical tools that allow us to see how one numerical variable behaves in relation to another. The most fundamental of these is the scatterplot, which represents each observation as a point in a two-dimensional space. This simple construction makes it possible to perceive patterns of association—whether variables tend to increase together, move in opposite directions, or show no clear relationship at all. In doing so, scatterplots extend the idea of distribution from a single dimension to a paired structure, where the focus is no longer on how values are distributed individually, but on how they co-vary.


2.2.1 Line Graph
In a related but distinct setting, we are often interested not only in relationships between variables, but in how a single variable evolves across an ordered structure such as time or sequence. In these cases, preserving order becomes essential, since the arrangement of observations carries meaning in itself. Line graphs are designed for this purpose. By connecting consecutive observations, they emphasize continuity and allow patterns such as trends, cycles, and gradual shifts to emerge clearly. Unlike scatterplots, where the order of points is irrelevant, line graphs rely on structure in the x-dimension to reveal how a variable changes over progression. Together, scatterplots and line graphs complete the visual framework of this chapter by extending analysis from distributions, to comparisons, and finally to relationships and change over order.
